title: Welcome to Our Community
excerpt: Discover what makes our senior living community a place where residents thrive every day.
content:
We are excited to welcome you to our community blog. Here, we will share stories, updates, tips, and insights about life in our senior living community.

From wellness programs and chef-prepared dining to social events and educational lectures, there is always something happening here. Our residents enjoy a vibrant, supportive environment where every day brings new opportunities for connection and growth.

Stay tuned for articles about senior health and wellness, family caregiver resources, community event recaps, and resident spotlights. We look forward to sharing our story with you.
author: Community Team

title: The Ultimate Oklahoma Moving Checklist
excerpt: Moving in Oklahoma? Use this step-by-step checklist to stay organized from eight weeks out to move-in day — including when to reserve your storage unit.
content:
8 Weeks Before Moving Day

Start your move organized — not panicked. Eight weeks out is the ideal time to begin decluttering and planning. Walk through every room with three boxes: keep, donate, and toss. Be ruthless. Everything you don't move is money saved on movers.

  • Inventory everything you own — photograph valuables for insurance
  • Research and book a moving company (summer moves book fast in OKC)
  • Reserve a storage unit if you'll need temporary or long-term storage
  • Request time off work for moving week
  • Notify your employer, bank, and key institutions of your upcoming address change

6 Weeks Out — Supplies & Admin

Start gathering quality packing supplies. Cheap boxes fail. Buy double-walled boxes from a moving supply store or ask your storage facility — OP Self Storage sells quality boxes at each location.

  • Stock up on boxes (small for heavy items, large for light items)
  • Buy packing tape, bubble wrap, and packing paper
  • Begin transferring utilities — electric, gas, internet, water
  • File a USPS change of address form
  • Update your Oklahoma driver's license address

4 Weeks Out — Pack Non-Essentials

Pack items you won't need in the next month: seasonal clothes, books, decorative items, hobby equipment. Label every box on the top and side with room and contents.

  • Pack off-season clothing first
  • Disassemble furniture you won't need before the move
  • Begin loading your storage unit with packed boxes
  • Confirm moving company details and get everything in writing

2 Weeks Out — The Home Stretch

Now pack the medium-priority items. Keep a "moving day essentials" box separate — this stays in your car, not the truck, and contains everything you'll need the first 24 hours.

  • Pack most kitchen items (keep a few pots, plates, and utensils)
  • Defrost and clean the refrigerator
  • Return library books, borrowed items, rented equipment
  • Confirm your storage unit access code and gate hours

Moving Day Tips

Oklahoma summers are brutal — hydrate constantly if you're moving June through September. Start early in the morning before the heat peaks. If using a storage unit, load heaviest boxes first and stack lighter items on top.

"The biggest moving mistake we see? People underestimate how much they own. When in doubt, reserve one size larger than you think you need — you can always downsize later." — OP Self Storage team

After You Move In

Once you're settled, don't forget your stored items. Visit your storage unit within the first two weeks to make sure everything is dry and secure. Update your account with your new address so billing and communications reach you.

Questions? Call any OP Self Storage location — our team is happy to walk you through packing best practices, unit sizing, and anything else that makes your move easier.
author: OP Self Storage Team

title: Business Storage in Oklahoma City: The Complete Guide
excerpt: From retail inventory to contractor equipment, Oklahoma City businesses are discovering that self-storage is smarter and cheaper than commercial warehouse space. Here's everything you need to know.
content:
Why Oklahoma Businesses Use Self-Storage

Oklahoma City commercial real estate is competitive. Warehouse space in the metro area starts at $8–12 per square foot annually — and that's before build-out costs, long-term lease commitments, and property taxes. A 10×20 climate-controlled storage unit at OP Self Storage costs a fraction of that, with month-to-month flexibility and zero build-out required.

We serve hundreds of OKC-area businesses, including restaurants storing seasonal equipment, contractors storing tools, retailers managing inventory overflow, and professional services firms archiving documents.

What Business Customers Store at OP

  • Retail inventory overflow — seasonal merchandise, backup stock, display fixtures
  • Contractor and trades equipment — power tools, ladders, materials, work vehicles
  • Restaurant and foodservice — smallwares, seasonal décor, catering equipment
  • Document archiving — paper records, legal files, financial documents (climate control prevents mold and degradation)
  • Medical and healthcare — non-perishable supplies, equipment, promotional materials
  • Real estate and staging — furniture, staging props, signage

OP Self Storage Business Features

We've designed our facilities with business customers in mind. Here's what sets us apart from residential-focused storage:

  • 24/7 gate access — pick up supplies before a job at 5am or after an event at midnight
  • Drive-up units — pull your vehicle directly to the unit door for fast loading and unloading
  • Package acceptance — we accept deliveries on your behalf during office hours
  • Flexible lease terms — month-to-month or discounted 6/12-month agreements
  • Business address service — use our address as a registered business address (call for details)
  • Multiple unit management — manage multiple units under one account with consolidated billing

Right-Sizing for Business Storage

The most common mistake business customers make is underestimating their needs. Here's a rough guide:

  • 10×10 (100 sq ft) — ideal for small retail overflow, documents, or a contractor's secondary tools
  • 10×15 (150 sq ft) — handles a mid-size retail inventory or small equipment collection
  • 10×20 (200 sq ft) — a full warehouse-style unit suitable for most small business needs
  • 10×30 (300 sq ft) — large commercial storage; accommodates pallet quantities and heavy equipment

Business Storage Tax Considerations

Storage rental fees paid for business purposes are generally tax-deductible as a business expense. Keep your monthly invoices — they're deductible on Schedule C or your entity's business return. Consult your CPA for guidance specific to your situation.

"We switched from a commercial warehouse to OP Self Storage and cut our storage costs by 60% with more flexibility. The 24/7 access was a game-changer for our catering business." — OKC catering company customer

Getting Started

Call us at (405) 555-0192 or visit any OP location to discuss your business storage needs. We'll assess your volume, recommend the right unit size, and can often have you moved in same day. Month-to-month leases mean you're never locked into more than you need.
author: OP Self Storage Team

title: Seasonal Storage Tips for Oklahoma Homeowners
excerpt: Oklahoma's four extreme seasons mean your home is constantly battling clutter from seasonal gear. Here's how to use storage strategically to keep your home organized year-round.
content:
Oklahoma's Four Seasons of Storage

Oklahoma experiences all four seasons with intensity — torrid summers, frigid winters, spectacular springs, and crisp autumns. Each season brings its own gear, decorations, and clothing requirements. Without a storage strategy, your garage and closets end up as chaotic layover zones for stuff that belongs somewhere else.

Spring Swap (February–April)

Spring is prime storage rotation time. As temperatures climb above 60°F, it's time to pull out lawn furniture, garden tools, and sports equipment — and put away the winter gear.

  • Move heavy coats, snow gear, and winter boots to storage
  • Pull out lawn furniture and inspect for winter damage before the season starts
  • Retrieve spring/summer sports gear: bikes, golf clubs, camping equipment
  • Store holiday decorations from Easter through Christmas in clearly labeled bins

Climate control matters in spring: Oklahoma spring means humidity spikes. Leather, wood, fabric, and electronics all suffer in uncontrolled humidity. OP's climate-controlled units manage humidity year-round.

Summer Prep (May)

Before the heat hits 100°F, make sure anything temperature-sensitive is in climate-controlled storage — not your garage, which can reach 130°F+ in July.

  • Never store vinyl records, candles, wine, photos, or electronics in non-climate-controlled spaces during summer
  • Store winter holiday décor in acid-free containers
  • Rotate summer clothing from storage; store heavier items

Fall Rotation (September–October)

Fall is the most satisfying storage swap. Bring out the cozy — and clear the summer clutter.

  • Drain and store water features, outdoor furniture, and irrigation lines before first freeze
  • Pull out holiday décor for Halloween and Thanksgiving
  • Retrieve cold-weather clothing and gear
  • Store summer sports equipment, swimwear, outdoor games

Winter Storage (November–January)

Oklahoma winters can bring ice storms and sub-zero temperatures. Protect seasonal items from freeze damage.

  • Store vehicles, motorcycles, and recreational vehicles in climate-controlled units
  • Keep holiday decorations in labeled, stackable bins for easy retrieval
  • Store delicate items (instruments, antiques, wine) where temperature is controlled

Packing for Long-Term Storage

Whether it's seasonal or indefinite, proper packing protects your belongings:

  • Use quality boxes — avoid banana boxes from grocery stores (bugs, moisture, weak walls)
  • Wrap fragile items in acid-free tissue or bubble wrap
  • Store mattresses in plastic mattress bags
  • Place moisture absorbers in bins with fabric or leather items
  • Label every box on the top and two sides
  • Keep a simple inventory spreadsheet — you'll thank yourself later

Need help with a unit size for your seasonal rotation? Call OP Self Storage at (405) 555-0192 — our team can size you up in minutes and get you moved in the same day.
author: OP Self Storage Team

title: Climate-Controlled Storage: Why It Matters in Oklahoma
excerpt: Oklahoma's extreme temperature swings can destroy belongings stored in non-climate-controlled units. Here's exactly what climate control does — and why every OP Self Storage unit has it.
content:
What Is Climate-Controlled Storage?

Climate-controlled storage maintains a consistent temperature range — typically 55–85°F — and manages relative humidity inside the unit. This is achieved through HVAC systems, insulation, and in some facilities, dedicated humidity control systems.

Standard (non-climate-controlled) storage units are essentially metal boxes exposed to outside temperatures. In Oklahoma, that means 120°F+ internal temperatures in summer and sub-freezing conditions in winter. The damage this causes is significant and often irreversible.

Oklahoma's Extreme Climate Challenge

Oklahoma City sees some of the most dramatic temperature swings in the United States:

  • Summer highs regularly exceed 100°F, with garage and storage interiors reaching 130°F+
  • Winter lows can drop below 0°F during ice events
  • Spring brings high humidity and rapid temperature shifts that cause condensation
  • Tornado season means pressure changes that can compromise non-insulated structures

These conditions are destructive. Materials expand and contract, wood warps, adhesives fail, and moisture condenses inside boxes and electronics.

What Gets Damaged in Non-Climate-Controlled Storage

  • Electronics: Heat degrades capacitors and batteries; condensation causes corrosion
  • Wood furniture: Expansion and contraction from temperature cycles causes warping, cracking, and joint failure
  • Photographs and documents: Heat yellows photos; humidity causes mold and adhesion
  • Clothing and fabric: Humidity causes mildew; heat sets stains permanently
  • Vinyl records and media: Heat warps records; humidity destroys media
  • Wine and collectibles: Temperature fluctuations ruin wine and damage collectible values
  • Musical instruments: Wood instruments are especially vulnerable to humidity swings

Why Every OP Unit Is Climate-Controlled

When Dale Patterson built the first OP Self Storage in 1999, he made a decision that set OP apart from most competitors: every unit in every facility would be climate-controlled. No exceptions, no tiers, no upcharges.

That decision wasn't just good for customers — it's become a defining advantage. Customers who've used non-climate-controlled storage elsewhere have horror stories. Our customers don't.

Climate Control vs. Temperature-Only Control

True climate control manages both temperature and humidity. Temperature-only units keep the heat and cold at bay, but high humidity can still cause mold, mildew, and corrosion. OP Self Storage units manage both, keeping relative humidity in the 30–50% range that's safe for virtually all common stored items.

Ready to protect your belongings properly? Reserve a climate-controlled unit at OP Self Storage — units start at $42/mo in Moore and $49/mo in Oklahoma City.
author: OP Self Storage Team
